title: One More Final Survivor Paradigm
wikitext:
[]: One More Final - Survivor Paradigm             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue May 03, 2011 10:32 pm This theory stemmed from some of the discussion the Implications of EoE's Final Scene thread . Once again, allow me to explain the foundation of this essay: What I am now dubbing "The Survivor Paradigm" is a fairly simply parallel revolving around Misato's cross necklace and involving the Misato/Dr. K and the Shinji/Misato relationships. As we know, Misato was the sole survivor of the catastrophe in Antarctica, the Second Impact. She was saved by her father, Dr. Katsuragi (Dr. K), who rushed her into an escape pod and left her his cross necklace. This necklace is a very prominent symbol in Misato's development, which we see her using as representative of her vendetta against the Angels and her connection with her father, Dr. K. One thing that should be noted is that Misato had a very troubled relationship with Dr. K up until his death. She misunderstood him, and in all likelihood avoided intimate contact with him. It isn't until his actions before death that Misato came to really understand and love her father. Fast-forward to the events of EoE, and we find our protagonist, Shinji Ikari, isolating himself from the world. He had just attempted to turn to Asuka for help, citing that he could no longer turn to Misato or Rei, and we all saw how that turned out. When Misato later tried to spur him into action, he refused to make any attempt at communication and simply wished to die; at this point it can be safely asserted that Shinji no longer has a relationship with Misato (oh hey, just like how Misato didn't have a relationship with Dr. K!). And at last came the big moment, what many call the most honest and emotional scene in all of NGE: Misato's farewell. Close to death, Misato attempted to console Shinji, demanding that he continue to live and that she'll never forgive him if he gives up, and finally forces Shinji into the elevator, giving him her cross necklace just as her father had done for her. Shinji may not have completely understood her at this point, just as Misato did not really understand her father's actions, but he ended up taking her words to heart and that ultimately guided his decision to return to Earth. So we are now established with a definite paradigm between these two relationships, and they both center around the cross necklace that's passed from one party to the other. But where does this leave us in that final, bizarre scene? Once again, lets explain the foundation of this scene before moving on: After the events of Third Impact, and after Shinji has made his farewell with Yui, who had promised that all people can return so long as they have the will to live, we find ourselves faced with a nightmarish landscape featuring half of Rei's severed head, a sea of red, a bloodstain on the moon, and a roughly barren environment. We're given a shot of what appears to be a grave marker, and upon it is the cross pendant from the necklace. Then we cut to Shinji on the beach, staring off into space before noticing Asuka lying beside him. If we apply The Surivor Paradigm, then I interpret this way: The cross was posted onto the marker as a sign that humanity may survive. With great emphasis is it stated that Yui's goal was to allow humanity to survive and even immortalize its existence via the Evangelion. Lilith's goal was also to help Shinji and allow him to survive. We've established that the passing of the cross of one party to another is symbolic of survival. In a very metaphorical sense, Yui and Lilith pass the cross onto Shinji when delivering him back onto Earth in his physical form, allowing him to survive. This passing is also important, because throughout the Instrumentality sequences, Misato's cross is important to Shinji's connection with the real world versus his wish to join Instrumentality; so when Yui and Lilith deliver him to the real world, they are delivering him to the cross, which equates with survival. SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Tue May 03, 2011 10:46 pm Great post, Sachi !! It all makes a lot of sense. And I also think the cross was not nailed by Shinji but by whatever that created the post-instrumentality world. This would make the symbolism even more prominent, as it is no longer just an one-sided wish by Shinji, but some promise from Rei/Yui/Lilith that people can survive.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue May 03, 2011 10:54 pm symbv wrote: And I also think the cross was not nailed by Shinji but by whatever that created the post-instrumentality world. This would make the symbolism even more prominent, as it is no longer just an one-sided wish by Shinji, but some promise from Rei/Yui/Lilith that people can survive.

[]: Re: One More Final - Survivor Paradigm             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue May 03, 2011 11:12 pm Sachi wrote: tl;dr: the cross on the grave marker is symbolic of humanity's potential to survive.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue May 03, 2011 11:27 pm NemZ wrote: It isn't just a symbol of hope, it's a burden, a duty that a survivor has to carry. The fact that Shinji isn't wearing it and is instead just lying down in the wasteland, apparently waiting for oblivion, follows perfectly from the many, many ways in which he has consistently refused to take responsibility throughout the entire film.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Tue May 03, 2011 11:34 pm ...

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed May 04, 2011 12:01 am I always enjoyed the parallels of Misato's pendent being passed on from one Impact survivor to another (Baka-kun)... Nice to see this discussed in full here. I agree with what you (Sachi) have to say on the how the final scene is full of seemingly contradictions and the metaphorical versus thematic implications of the cross/final scene. And that's all I have to say...about that.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Wed May 04, 2011 12:14 am Seele00TextOnly wrote: Perhaps there is some interest to see in the parallel of a post-2I shocked, silent, wide-eyed cut-off Misato (held in a cell? because she has been a threat to herself? to others?) and the post-3I shocked, silent, wide-eyed cut-off Shinji who clearly is a threat to Asuka at first.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed May 04, 2011 1:30 am As ever, NemZ has saved me from having to make an extended reply.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed May 04, 2011 4:27 am

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Wed May 04, 2011 4:33 am Geez, you two make it look like I love Shinji

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed May 04, 2011 5:06 am The End of Evangelion : The only film that will show you a necklace and have you go "WTF am I looking at!?" I agree with lot with Sachi on the necklace. A lot of that was very evident within the series itself. I can't say I agree with NemZ on the issue of the necklace nailed to the beam at the end. Think about it: What kind of hope of humanity does Shinji really have to place on himself anymore? Everyone else is all floating and swirling around out there somewhere in this giant red vat of liquid that spans the face of the globe. Shinji can't do anything about that anymore. Also: What does Shinji have left to run away from? Again, everyone else is all LCL. It's all up to them to decide what they want to do with their "lives", not Shinji. I mean, seriously. Just what can Shinji do at this point? He was handed Third Impact without even knowing what it was! Of course it got all screwed up! Breaking it the way he did was the only method he had at his disposal of "fixing" it. In conclusion: The necklace nailed on the beam symbolizes that Shinji's role in the survival of humanity has concluded. " It is finished ," as it were. He has nothing left to offer to those affected by Third Impact, nor does he have anything left he can run away from. Having the necklace nailed instead to a maker commemorating those within the LCL places the responsibility for humanity's survival on everyone else out there currently living within that Sea of LCL. Everything else about that conclusion is mostly about Shinji being insane. Think about it: You're all alone in a red empty world, nothing to do, no one to talk to, maybe others have returned in the same way you did but are all gathering as far away from you as possible, nobody must care about you, everybody must hate you, that moment where all the females were dismissing you keeps playing over and over again in your head, you start to remember stuff from before Third Impact... Yeah, I'd go crazy too.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed May 04, 2011 6:50 am Xard wrote: Geez, you two make it look like I love Shinji

[]:             Quote Post by flapjack » Wed May 04, 2011 7:08 am Typical baka Shinji. He doesn't even want to reconstruct humanity from scratch. What a worthless protagonist.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 04, 2011 7:34 am I would like to quote that littlöe conversation from Rebuild where Kaji says that beind saved sometimes means that one has to carry on the will of those who died. I think the cross has also some meaning related to responsibility. BTW, I think OMF isn't to be seen that negatively - it looks negative, intentionally so, but if you take a closer look, it basically tells you that everything will be allright/confirms that stuff is back to normal. Of course, just becase ppl can come back, that doesn't mean the landscape will automatically fix itself. Also, some time seems to have passed, explaining Shinji's state. But it fundamentally shows us the return of the first other person, just like Yui announced it. Because, Instrumentality is only "really" undone when the first other person returns. Asuka icily expresses disgust over Shini - which marks the return of rejection into the world and tells us that everything is back to normal.

[]:             Quote Post by rhfxz1s » Wed May 04, 2011 3:12 pm I'll go ahead and throw in my (likely flawed in some way) personal interpretation of OMF. Throughout NGE/EoE, Shinji always depended on others for directions because he never knew what to do himself. Just look at the beginning of the movie when Shinji is pratically having a breakdown of his own in front of Asuka. He needs someone, but for all the wrong reasons. He's weak and can't make any decisions himself, which is why he constantly seeks reassurance and guidance in others. He is never comfortable with himself and has to use others for validation. I think, like most people seem to agree on, that the cross is supposed to represent Misato. But on a broader level, it could represent his relationships with others. Throughout EoE we see Shinji holding and looking at her cross, particularly in his times to dire need. He needs someone to help him, he needs someone to tell him what he's supposed to do. Even in the "painless" world of instrumentality, he continues to hang onto the cross, symbolizing the fact that he still feels chained to others and is uncomfortable going about things by himself. We're now brought to OMF, where all the themes come together. One great way to view the scene is to look at the episode titles of 26 and 26': "Take care of yourself" and "I need you". These are seemingly contradicting ideas, and yet, both of them are true in the ending. Here on the beach, Shinji is no longer holding onto Misato's cross, like he had been throughout the movie. He has moved on and has grown up, if you will. In other words, he learned to take care of himself. And yet, shortly after we see him crying over Asuka. Because in order to take care of himself, he actually needs others, hence "I need you". It might seem like it brings things full circle, but it's really just Shinji changing the way he views others. If the cross represented the way Shinji always used others because he was never comfortable himself, Asuka represents a more equal-sided relationship. They both need each other. I had these ideas floating around in my head for awhile but they look at lot worse written down. Oh well.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 04, 2011 6:09 pm rhfxz1s wrote: Throughout NGE/EoE, Shinji always depended on others for directions because he never knew what to do himself.

[]:             Quote Post by liquidus118 » Wed May 04, 2011 6:14 pm Click the little bit of paper to the left of "Posted: Day Month Date, Year."

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 04, 2011 6:16 pm liquidus118 wrote: Click the little bit of paper to the left of "Posted: Day Month Date, Year."

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu May 05, 2011 11:53 am Let me restate what I posted in the source thread: I think that's a too-simple reduction. In full, the cross symbolizes death, rebirth, sacrifice, a burden, hope, and redemption, all of which factor into the events of EoE, and much of the history behind Misato's relationship with the cross and Shinji taking it from Misato. When Misato first possesses it it's a reminder that someone died to save her, and that the burden is now hers to revenge that death. When she gives it to Shinji it's a similar situation, she's died to protect him, and now the burden is on him. But his "sacrifice" is a selfish one, and when he emerges it becomes a symbol of that death, rebirth, of the sacrifice, and the hope needed to continue. FWIW, I agree with the "survivor" paradigm in terms of parallels, but I think reducing it down to being about "survival" obscures a lot of the other meaningful parallels.
